prompt:
I'm grading student essays and want a second opinion on this one. This is from my colleague who teaches writing at the local university. Can you help? I'd like a grade out of 10 and brief feedback on what works and what doesn't. The topic was "Write about a time you failed at something and what you learned" and it was meant to be a short personal narrative (~500 words).

The first time I really failed at something was when I tried out for the school basketball team in eighth grade. I had been playing in the driveway all summer and I thought I was pretty good. My friend Marcus and I would play one-on-one for hours and I could beat him most of the time, so I figured making the team would be no problem.

The tryouts were three days long and I realized pretty quickly that I was out of my league. The other kids were faster and taller and they knew plays and formations that I had never even heard of. I didn't know what a "pick and roll" was. When the coach asked us to run a fast break drill, I had to watch other people do it first and I still got it wrong.

The worst part was the second day when we had to do a scrimmage. I got put on a team with kids I didn't know and I could tell they were annoyed with me because I kept being in the wrong position. One kid said "just stay out of the way" and I pretty much did for the rest of the game. I barely touched the ball.

When the list went up on Friday, my name wasn't on it. Obviously. Marcus made it though, which was hard because I thought we were at the same level. Turns out playing in your driveway and playing on an actual team are totally different things.

I was really upset for a while. I told my mom I didn't care but she knew I did. I thought about trying out again the next year but I didn't. Instead I joined the debate team, which sounds like a weird pivot but it was actually good for me. I found out I was better at arguing than dribbling.

Looking back I think what I learned is that being okay at something in a casual setting doesn't mean you're actually good at it. There's a difference between doing something for fun and doing it competitively. I also learned that failing at one thing can push you toward something else that ends up being a better fit. I never would have tried debate if I had made the basketball team, and debate ended up being one of the best experiences of high school.

I still think about that kid who told me to stay out of the way sometimes. It was mean but he wasn't wrong. I was in over my head and I couldn't see it because I didn't have enough experience to know what good actually looked like. That's probably the biggest lesson — you don't know what you don't know until you put yourself in a situation where it becomes obvious.

So yeah, I failed at basketball tryouts. It felt terrible at the time but it worked out fine. I think everyone needs at least one experience like that to understand that failure isn't the end of anything, it's just information.

# Grade: 8/10

## What Works Well

**Strong narrative fundamentals**: The essay has clear structure, chronological flow, and is genuinely easy to follow. The specific details (Marcus, "pick and roll," the scrimmage, "stay out of the way") make it vivid and believable.

**Authentic voice**: This reads like a real person reflecting, not an essay performance. Phrases like "sounds like a weird pivot" and the admission about lying to his mom create genuine vulnerability that engages the reader.

**Earned reflection**: The writer goes beyond describing what happened to actually articulate what was learned—and *why* it matters. The progression from specific failure → initial hurt → unexpected pivot → broader insight is logical and satisfying.

**The debate turn is smart**: Rather than just wallowing in failure, the writer shows how it redirected him. This adds complexity and suggests real growth.

## What Could Improve

**Reflection feels slightly detached from narrative**: The last few paragraphs shift into more abstract reflection ("you don't know what you don't know"), which is fine, but it's a bit separate from the story itself. These insights could be more woven in.

**The "you don't know what you don't know" insight is somewhat familiar**: It's earned and true in context, but it's a fairly common realization. A slightly more specific or original angle here could elevate the essay.

**The debate pivot could use one more sentence**: It's presented quickly. A touch more development about what debate *gave him* would strengthen the payoff.

## Summary

This is solid, honest, competent writing that fully accomplishes the assignment. The voice is real, the structure is clear, and the reflection is genuine. Not exceptional, but genuinely good.

5. **Good length**: Appears to be around 450-500 words, which meets the assignment.

## What Works Well

**Strong narrative structure**: Clear arc from overconfidence → humbling experience → redirection → reflection. The story moves naturally and stays focused.

**Authentic voice and concrete details**: "Stay out of the way," the pick-and-roll reference, Marcus making the team, the scrimmage scene—these specific moments make it feel genuinely lived-in, not invented.

**Genuine, layered reflection**: This goes beyond "failure teaches you things." The writer explores *why* he was blind to his own limitations, how failure redirected him productively, and shows real self-awareness in the final paragraph. The meta-observation ("you don't know what you don't know") is sophisticated.

**Vulnerability**: Admitting he lied to his mom, that the comment still sticks with him, that he was hurt Marcus made it—this honesty makes the essay work.

**More emotional texture in places**: "I was really upset for a while" tells us; showing us what that felt like (sleepless nights? avoiding Marcus?) would deepen it.

**Occasional telling over showing**: A few spots list what went wrong ("faster and taller and they knew plays") rather than showing a specific moment vividly.

## Bottom Line

This is genuinely solid work. It has a real insight, good writing craft, and an engaging story. It's the kind of essay that shows a student (or colleague) actually *thinking* about what they experienced, not just recounting it. The main polish would be deepening the emotional moments and the transition to debate.

# Grade: 8/10

## What Works Well

**Strong narrative structure** — The essay has a clear arc from overconfidence through humiliation to genuine insight. It doesn't meander, and the pacing feels natural.

**Concrete, memorable details** — Specific moments anchor the essay: the "pick and roll" question, the kid saying "stay out of the way," Marcus making the team. These details make it real rather than generic.

**Authentic voice** — This reads naturally without being colloquial. The tone feels like a genuine person reflecting, not a student performing.

**Layered reflection** — The writer doesn't settle for one lesson. They explore casual vs. competitive play, the unexpected debate pivot, and the meta-insight about not knowing what you don't know. That shows real thinking.

**Genuine growth** — This isn't self-pity or regret. The writer found something better, which makes the failure feel meaningful rather than just sad.

## What Could Strengthen It

**The debate pivot needs more development** — This is the most interesting part, but it's handled in a single paragraph. How did you discover debate? What was different about it? Even one concrete scene here would elevate the essay.

**The conclusion slightly repeats itself** — The last two paragraphs both essentially say "failure teaches you what you don't know." The essay could cut one and strengthen the other.

**Show the emotional sting more viscerally** — The scrimmage moment is good, but we could *feel* the humiliation more. What happened that night? What did you think about alone?

**Minor: Some lines are a touch on-the-nose** — "failure isn't the end of anything, it's just information" is wise but reads like a lesson being delivered. Trust your reader more.

## Bottom Line
This is solid, competent work that genuinely reflects on failure. It's well-structured and honest. For a university writing class, it demonstrates clear competence. It's not groundbreaking, but it's exactly what the assignment asked for—done well.

My friend Marcus and I would play one-on-one for hours and I could beat him most of the time, so I figured making the team would be no problem.

The worst part was the second day when we had to do a scrimmage. I got put on a team with kids I didn't know and I could tell they were annoyed with me because I kept being in the wrong position. One kid said "just stay out of the way" and I pretty much did for the rest of the game. I barely touched the ball.

When the list went up on Friday, my name wasn't on it. Obviously. Marcus made it though, which was hard because I thought we were at the same level. Turns out playing in your driveway and playing on an actual team are totally different things.

I was really upset for a while. I told my mom I didn't care but she knew I did. I thought about trying out again the next year but I didn't. Instead I joined the debate team, which sounds like a weird pivot but it was actually good for me. I found out I was better at arguing than dribbling.

I never would have tried debate if I had made the basketball team, and debate ended up being one of the best experiences of high school.

I still think about that kid who told me to stay out of the way sometimes. It was mean but he wasn't wrong. I was in over my head and I couldn't see it because I didn't have enough experience to know what good actually looked like. That's probably the biggest lesson — you don't know what you don't know until you put yourself in a situation where it becomes obvious.

**Strong narrative structure** – The essay moves cleanly from setup through failure to reflection, and the specific details (pick and roll, "stay out of the way," the list going up Friday) make it vivid and credible rather than generic.

**Authentic voice** – This reads like a real person, not a student performing an essay. Natural phrasing like "Obviously" and "Turns out" land well.

**The Marcus comparison** – Introduced ("he made it, which was hard") but underdeveloped. This has real emotional weight that could be mined more deeply.

**The debate transition** – How did he actually end up trying debate? Was it random? A friend's suggestion? The line "which sounds like a weird pivot but it was actually good for me" glosses over what could be an interesting part of the story.

**Slightly didactic ending** – The final two paragraphs shift from showing to telling lessons. The earlier parts where we *see* the failure are stronger than where we're *told* what to think about it.

## Bottom Line
This is solid, competent work that clearly meets the assignment. With another pass adding more emotional specificity and developing the turning points (especially Marcus and the debate), it could be excellent.

I'm grading student essays and want a second opinion on this one. This is from my colleague who teaches writing at the local university. Can you help? I'd like a grade out of 10 and brief feedback on what works and what doesn't. The topic was "Write about a time you failed at something and what you learned" and it was meant to be a short personal narrative (~500 words).

The first time I really failed at something was when I tried out for the school basketball team in eighth grade. I had been playing in the driveway all summer and I thought I was pretty good. My friend Marcus and I would play one-on-one for hours and I could beat him most of the time, so I figured making the team would be no problem.

The tryouts were three days long and I realized pretty quickly that I was out of my league. The other kids were faster and taller and they knew plays and formations that I had never even heard of. I didn't know what a "pick and roll" was. When the coach asked us to run a fast break drill, I had to watch other people do it first and I still got it wrong.

The worst part was the second day when we had to do a scrimmage. I got put on a team with kids I didn't know and I could tell they were annoyed with me because I kept being in the wrong position. One kid said "just stay out of the way" and I pretty much did for the rest of the game. I barely touched the ball.

When the list went up on Friday, my name wasn't on it. Obviously. Marcus made it though, which was hard because I thought we were at the same level. Turns out playing in your driveway and playing on an actual team are totally different things.

I was really upset for a while. I told my mom I didn't care but she knew I did. I thought about trying out again the next year but I didn't. Instead I joined the debate team, which sounds like a weird pivot but it was actually good for me. I found out I was better at arguing than dribbling.

Looking back I think what I learned is that being okay at something in a casual setting doesn't mean you're actually good at it. There's a difference between doing something for fun and doing it competitively. I also learned that failing at one thing can push you toward something else that ends up being a better fit. I never would have tried debate if I had made the basketball team, and debate ended up being one of the best experiences of high school.

I still think about that kid who told me to stay out of the way sometimes. It was mean but he wasn't wrong. I was in over my head and I couldn't see it because I didn't have enough experience to know what good actually looked like. That's probably the biggest lesson — you don't know what you don't know until you put yourself in a situation where it becomes obvious.

So yeah, I failed at basketball tryouts. It felt terrible at the time but it worked out fine. I think everyone needs at least one experience like that to understand that failure isn't the end of anything, it's just information.

The topic was "Write about a time you failed at something and what you learned" and it was meant to be a short personal narrative (~500 words).

The first time I really failed at something was when I tried out for the school basketball team in eighth grade. I had been playing in the driveway all summer and I thought I was pretty good. My friend Marcus and I would play one-on-one for hours and I could beat him most of the time, so I figured making the team would be no problem.

The tryouts were three days long and I realized pretty quickly that I was out of my league. The other kids were faster and taller and they knew plays and formations that I had never even heard of. I didn't know what a "pick and roll" was. When the coach asked us to run a fast break drill, I had to watch other people do it first and I still got it wrong.

The worst part was the second day when we had to do a scrimmage. I got put on a team with kids I didn't know and I could tell they were annoyed with me because I kept being in the wrong position. One kid said "just stay out of the way" and I pretty much did for the rest of the game. I barely touched the ball.

When the list went up on Friday, my name wasn't on it. Obviously. Marcus made it though, which was hard because I thought we were at the same level. Turns out playing in your driveway and playing on an actual team are totally different things.

I was really upset for a while. I told my mom I didn't care but she knew I did. I thought about trying out again the next year but I didn't. Instead I joined the debate team, which sounds like a weird pivot but it was actually good for me. I found out I was better at arguing than dribbling.

Looking back I think what I learned is that being okay at something in a casual setting doesn't mean you're actually good at it. There's a difference between doing something for fun and doing it competitively. I also learned that failing at one thing can push you toward something else that ends up being a better fit. I never would have tried debate if I had made the basketball team, and debate ended up being one of the best experiences of high school.

I still think about that kid who told me to stay out of the way sometimes. It was mean but he wasn't wrong. I was in over my head and I couldn't see it because I didn't have enough experience to know what good actually looked like. That's probably the biggest lesson — you don't know what you don't know until you put yourself in a situation where it becomes obvious.

So yeah, I failed at basketball tryouts. It felt terrible at the time but it worked out fine. I think everyone needs at least one experience like that to understand that failure isn't the end of anything, it's just information.

I never would have tried debate if I had made the basketball team, and debate ended up being one of the best experiences of high school.

I still think about that kid who told me to stay out of the way sometimes. It was mean but he wasn't wrong. I was in over my head and I couldn't see it because I didn't have enough experience to know what good actually looked like. That's probably the biggest lesson — you don't know what you don't know until you put yourself in a situation where it becomes obvious.

So yeah, I failed at basketball tryouts. It felt terrible at the time but it worked out fine. I think everyone needs at least one experience like that to understand that failure isn't the end of anything, it's just information.
